title: Digitisation: original, microfilm and digital surrogate
intro: Digitisation tempts people to create a new record for every copy. RiC says otherwise: the content is one Record, and the original, the microfilm and the digital scan are all Instantiations of it — with derivation links between them. This scenario models that surrogate chain.
question: Is a digital scan a new Record, or an Instantiation of the same Record? How do I link surrogates?

steps.prompt: Start with the document being reformatted.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. The document's content is one Record. Every physical and digital copy will be an Instantiation of this single Record.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: That's a carrier (the paper, the film, the file). The intellectual content is the Record they all instantiate.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Not for a single document — a Set aggregates many records.

capture.comment: Create the document as a Record.

steps.prompt: You scan the document. Is the digital scan a new Record, or something else?

choices.label: An Instantiation of the same Record
choices.why: Fits — and it's the key point. A surrogate carries the SAME content, so it's another Instantiation of the one Record (linked with has_instantiation), not a new Record. That keeps every copy under one intellectual description.

choices.label: A new Record
choices.why: Doesn't fit. A scan isn't new content — it's a new carrier of existing content. Making it a Record duplicates the description and breaks the link to the original.

choices.label: A Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit — the scan isn't a component that adds content; it re-presents the whole document. It's an Instantiation.

capture.comment: Create the digital scan as an Instantiation of the document.
Link the document to its digital scan with has_instantiation.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: Fits. The original paper is also an Instantiation of the Record — the source one. Now the Record has two Instantiations (original + scan), each with its own format and condition.

choices.label: The Record itself
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The Record is the intellectual content; the paper is one carrier of it — an Instantiation.

capture.comment: Create the original paper as an Instantiation.
Link the document to the original with has_instantiation.

steps.prompt: The scan was made FROM the original. How do you record that the scan derives from it?

choices.label: Link them with has_derived_instantiation
choices.why: Fits. Relate the original Instantiation to the scan with has_derived_instantiation — capturing the reformatting lineage (and likewise for a microfilm intermediate). Now you can answer 'what was this scan made from?' and trust the chain.

choices.label: Nothing — both just hang off the Record
choices.why: Misses the lineage. Both are Instantiations of the Record, but the derivation (scan FROM original) is itself meaningful for provenance and preservation — record it with has_derived_instantiation.

choices.label: Make the scan a copy Record of the original
choices.why: Doesn't fit — surrogates aren't records. The derivation is between Instantiations.

capture.comment: Record that the original yielded the digital scan as a derived instantiation.

outcome.verdict: One Record, many carriers, lineage intact.
outcome.summary: The document is a single Record; the original, microfilm and digital scan are Instantiations of it; and has_derived_instantiation captures which surrogate came from which. Reformatting no longer fragments the description into duplicate records — it enriches one record with a traceable carrier chain.

links.label: See real RiC data in the graph viewer
